title: fetch — make your first HTTP request
instruction:
`fetch(url)` returns a Promise that resolves to a `Response` object. Call `.json()` on it to parse the body.

Two awaits:
1. `await fetch(url)` — wait for the response headers
2. `await response.json()` — wait for the body to be read

Fetch a user from the JSONPlaceholder test API.
successMessage: Two awaits — response headers first, then body. response.ok is true for 2xx status codes. This is the basic fetch pattern you'll use hundreds of times.

title: Error handling — network vs HTTP errors
instruction:
`fetch` only rejects for **network failures** (no connection, DNS failure). HTTP errors (404, 500) still resolve — you get a Response with `ok: false`.

You must check `response.ok` manually:

```js
if (!response.ok) throw new Error(`HTTP ${response.status}`);
```
successMessage: fetch doesn't throw for 404 — you must check response.ok. This is the most common fetch mistake. Always wrap fetch with a helper that checks ok and throws.

title: POST — send data
instruction:
POST requests send data in the body. You need to:
1. Set `method: 'POST'`
2. Set `Content-Type: application/json` header
3. JSON.stringify the body

Create a new post on JSONPlaceholder.
successMessage: POST with JSON body — the pattern for every form submission, API call, and data creation. JSONPlaceholder returns a fake ID of 101 (it doesn't actually save anything).

title: Query parameters with URLSearchParams
successMessage: URLSearchParams handles encoding — spaces become +, special characters are percent-encoded. Never concatenate query strings manually — it's error-prone and a security risk.

title: Request headers and Authorization
successMessage: A reusable API client with auth headers baked in. This is the pattern real apps use — Axios, SWR, React Query all wrap fetch with helpers like this.

title: AbortController — cancel requests
instruction:
`AbortController` cancels in-flight requests — essential for:
- Search-as-you-type (cancel old request when user types more)
- Component unmount (cancel if user navigates away)
- Timeout (cancel after N milliseconds)

Build a fetch with timeout using AbortController.
hint: const controller = new AbortController(); fetch(url, { signal: controller.signal }); setTimeout(() => controller.abort(), ms);
successMessage: AbortController + clearTimeout — the proper fetch timeout pattern. React's useEffect hook returns a cleanup function that should call controller.abort() when the component unmounts.

title: Parallel requests with Promise.all
instruction:
From Chapter 17: `Promise.all` runs Promises in parallel. Applied to fetch: load multiple API resources simultaneously.

Fetch user data and their posts in parallel, then combine them.
successMessage: Two requests in parallel — takes as long as the slower one, not both combined. This is how dashboards load multiple data sources efficiently.

title: Build a mini HTTP client library
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build a `createHttpClient(baseUrl, options)` factory that:
- `get(path, params)` — GET request with optional query params
- `post(path, data)` — POST with JSON body
- `put(path, data)` — PUT with JSON body
- `delete(path)` — DELETE
- Auto-handles `response.ok` check and throws on errors
- Supports `options.headers` for default headers (e.g. auth)
- Supports `options.timeout` for request cancellation

Test against JSONPlaceholder.
hint: Return different fetch configurations based on method. Use AbortController for timeout.
successMessage: A complete HTTP client library — headers, timeout, all methods, error handling. This is what Axios does under the hood. Chapter 21 done!
